reasoning:
The current spread is 2.58%, representing an extreme short-term low following a strong downward trend from 2.83% a month ago. The FRED linear model estimates only a 36% chance of the spread exceeding 2.58% by January 25, driven by this persistent tightening. Additionally, because the resolution criteria require a strict increase, short-term forecasts must account for a non-trivial probability of a tie, which further suppresses the probability of a "YES" resolution over very short, 7-day horizons. 

However, looking further ahead to February 17, market consensus and macroeconomic outlooks strongly favor mean reversion. Credit spreads are currently at multi-year (cycle) tight levels, and major banks project high-yield spreads in Europe will widen out to ~280-300 bps by the end of 2026 due to maturing credit cycles, potential geopolitical uncertainty, and standard mean reversion. As the time horizon expands, the dampening effect of a tie diminishes, and the upward pull of long-term spread normalization begins to counteract the short-term downward momentum. Therefore, the probability of an increase converges back closer to 0.5 for the later date in February, fully aligning with the instruction that uncertainty expands and probabilities draw nearer to 50% for distant dates.
